title: Chronic Kidney Disease
authors: Joanne M. Bargman, Karl L. Skorecki

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF CKD
items.title: Mechanisms of Chronic Kidney Disease Progression
items.content: CKD progression involves specific initiating mechanisms related to the underlying etiology and nonspecific mechanisms like hyperfiltration and hypertrophy. The latter are common consequences of long-term reduction in renal mass, leading to maladaptive changes such as glomerular architecture distortion, abnormal podocyte function, and sclerosis.
keyPoints: Two broad mechanisms: specific initiating mechanisms (e.g., genetic abnormalities, immune complex deposition) and nonspecific mechanisms (hyperfiltration, hypertrophy).
Hyperfiltration and hypertrophy are responses to reduced nephron number mediated by vasoactive hormones, cytokines, growth factors.
Increased intrarenal activity of the renin-angiotensin system contributes to compensatory hyperfiltration and maladaptive hypertrophy/sclerosis.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Chronic Kidney Disease | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF CKD

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF CKD
items.title: Staging and Risk Assessment in Chronic Kidney Disease
items.content: CKD staging is based on glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and urinary albumin excretion. The risk of further decline in GFR depends on these parameters, with stage 5 CKD indicating end-stage renal disease requiring renal replacement therapy.
keyPoints: Staging of CKD involves estimated risk for progressive decline based on GFR and urinary albumin excretion.
Stage 5 CKD represents end-stage renal disease necessitating dialysis or kidney transplantation.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Chronic Kidney Disease | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF CKD

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)
items.title: Monitoring and Management of Risk Factors for CKD
items.content: Adults with risk factors for chronic kidney disease (CKD) should be monitored annually to detect early signs of kidney impairment. This includes checking urinary albumin excretion, estimated GFR decline, and blood pressure levels.
keyPoints: Risk factors include tobacco use, increased BMI, sedentary lifestyle, past AKI, and childhood kidney disease recovery.
Genetic risk factors may account for up to 20% of adult-onset CKD.
Monitoring should focus on urinary albumin excretion, eGFR decline, and blood pressure.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Contributions to CKD
items.content: Genetic factors play a significant role in the development of CKD, with some mutations following Mendelian inheritance patterns. High penetrance mutations and partial penetrance variants can contribute to CKD, often requiring an acquired second hit for disease manifestation.
keyPoints: Up to 20% of adult-onset CKD may be due to genetic factors.
Common genetic contributors include APOL1 gene variants in sub-Saharan African ancestry populations.
Genetic mutations can affect podocytes or tubule segments, leading to primary or secondary kidney injury.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)
items.title: Staging of CKD Based on GFR and Urinary Albumin Excretion
items.content: CKD staging is crucial for understanding symptom emergence, progression risk, and treatment indications. It relies on eGFR and urinary albumin excretion rate, with race-free equations recommended for eGFR estimation.
keyPoints: Staging involves GFR and urinary albumin excretion rate assessment.
eGFR is estimated using serum creatinine and patient-specific parameters.
Race-free eGFR equations are now recommended, though adjustments may be needed in specific clinical scenarios.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 1)

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Age-Related Decline in GFR
items.content: The normal annual decline in glomerular filtration rate (GFR) with age is approximately 1 mL/min per year per 1.73 m², starting from a peak of ~120 mL/min at the third decade of life to about 70 mL/min by age 70. This decline signifies actual kidney function loss and necessitates medication dose adjustments.
keyPoints: Normal annual mean decline in GFR is ~1 mL/min per year per 1.73 m².
Peak GFR (~120 mL/min per 1.73 m²) occurs during the third decade of life.
Mean GFR at age 70 is approximately 70 mL/min per 1.73 m².
GFR decline with age indicates true kidney function loss and impacts medication dosing.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 2)

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 2)
items.title: Role of Albuminuria in Monitoring CKD
items.content: Albuminuria, measured by urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR), is crucial for detecting nephron injury and monitoring therapy response. A UACR >2.5 mg/mmol in males or >3.5 mg/mmol in females on multiple occasions indicates early kidney disease and systemic microvascular issues.
keyPoints: Albuminuria helps monitor nephron injury and therapy response, especially in chronic glomerular diseases.
UACR is preferred over 24-h urine collection for assessing glomerular injury.
Persistent UACR >2.5 mg/mmol (male) or >3.5 mg/mmol (female) indicates early kidney disease and systemic microvascular disease.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 2)

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF RISK FACTORS AND STAGING OF CKD (part 2)
items.title: Staging of CKD and Clinical Implications
items.content: Stages 1 and 2 CKD are often asymptomatic, detected through lab tests. Stages 3 and 4 present more clinical complications like anemia and electrolyte imbalances. Patients with stable eGFR and no proteinuria can be monitored without nephrology referral unless GFR declines or hypertension is uncontrolled.
keyPoints: Stages 1 and 2 CKD are usually asymptomatic, detected via lab tests.
Stages 3 and 4 CKD involve complications like anemia, electrolyte imbalances, and malnutrition.
Patients with stable eGFR and no proteinuria can be monitored without nephrology referral unless GFR declines or hypertension is uncontrolled.

items.title: Prevalence and Primary Causes of CKD in the US
items.content: Chronic kidney disease affects a significant portion of the adult population, with stages 1 and 2 affecting at least 6% and stages 3 and 4 affecting an additional 4.5%. Diabetic nephropathy is the most frequent cause of CKD in North America and Europe.
keyPoints: At least 6% of US adults have CKD stages 1 and 2.
An additional 4.5% have CKD stages 3 and 4.
Diabetic nephropathy is the most frequent cause of CKD in North America and Europe.

items.title: Understanding CKD in the Absence of Primary Glomerular or Tubulointerstitial Disease
items.content: In patients with newly diagnosed CKD and no overt primary kidney disease, hypertension is often a contributing factor. However, some may have subclinical glomerulopathy or systemic vascular disease affecting kidneys.
keyPoints: CKD without primary kidney disease often attributed to hypertension.
Subclinical primary glomerulopathy can cause elevated blood pressure as a consequence of kidney disease.
Systemic vascular diseases involving large and small vessels may contribute to CKD.

items.title: Complexities of the Uremic Syndrome in CKD
items.content: Uremia involves more than just renal excretory failure, affecting metabolic and endocrine functions. It includes systemic inflammation contributing to malnutrition-inflammation-atherosclerosis/calcification syndrome.
keyPoints: Uremia is not solely due to accumulation of urea and creatinine.
CKD affects metabolic and endocrine functions, leading to anemia, malnutrition, and abnormal metabolism.
Systemic inflammation in CKD contributes to vascular disease and morbidity.

items.subtopic: FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)
items.title: Management of Sodium Retention in CKD
items.content: In chronic kidney disease (CKD), impaired sodium excretion leads to retention and extracellular fluid volume expansion. This can contribute to hypertension and further nephron injury. Management includes salt restriction, use of thiazide diuretics like chlorthalidone for blood pressure control even in stage 4 CKD, and potentially higher doses or combinations with metolazone for loop diuretic resistance.
keyPoints: Sodium retention leads to extracellular fluid volume expansion and hypertension.
Thiazide diuretics like chlorthalidone can reduce blood pressure in stage 4 CKD.
Loop diuretics may require higher doses or combination with metolazone for resistance.
Diuretic resistance with edema and hypertension may indicate the need for dialysis.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)
items.title: Potassium Homeostasis in CKD
items.content: CKD patients often maintain urinary potassium excretion despite declining GFR due to aldosterone-dependent secretion and GI tract excretion. Hyperkalemia can occur with increased dietary intake, hemolysis, transfusion of stored red blood cells, metabolic acidosis, or medications like RAS inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics. Management includes cautious use of RAS inhibitors with monitoring and coadministration of potassium-lowering agents.
keyPoints: Urinary potassium excretion is maintained by aldosterone-dependent secretion and GI tract excretion.
Hyperkalemia can be triggered by dietary intake, hemolysis, transfusions, metabolic acidosis, or medications like RAS inhibitors.
RAS inhibitors are beneficial for CKD progression but require close monitoring of plasma potassium.
Coadministration with potassium-lowering agents reduces hyperkalemia risk.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)
items.title: Handling Hypovolemia in CKD
items.content: CKD patients may experience extrarenal fluid loss leading to extracellular fluid volume depletion. This can compromise kidney function through hypoperfusion or a prerenal state, potentially causing acute-on-chronic kidney failure. Management includes holding or adjusting diuretic doses and cautious volume repletion with normal saline.
keyPoints: Extrarenal fluid loss can lead to extracellular fluid volume depletion in CKD.
Volume depletion may cause hypoperfusion and prerenal acute-on-chronic kidney failure.
Management includes holding or adjusting diuretics and cautious volume repletion with normal saline.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 1)

items.subtopic: FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 2) | Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Disorders
items.title: Management of Metabolic Acidosis in CKD
items.content: Metabolic acidosis is prevalent in chronic kidney disease (CKD), often presenting as hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis due to decreased ammonia production. As CKD progresses, anion-gap metabolic acidosis can develop from retained organic acids. Mild metabolic acidosis typically has a pH >7.3 and can be managed with oral sodium bicarbonate supplementation.
keyPoints: Metabolic acidosis is common in CKD, often hyperchloremic due to reduced ammonia production.
Anion-gap metabolic acidosis may occur as CKD progresses.
Mild metabolic acidosis usually has a pH >7.3.
Oral sodium bicarbonate can correct mild metabolic acidosis.
Bicarbonate supplementation is advised when serum bicarbonate <20–23 mmol/L to prevent protein catabolism and slow CKD progression.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 2) | Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Disorders

items.subtopic: FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 2) | Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Disorders
items.title: Approach to Hyperkalemia in CKD
items.content: Hyperkalemia is a frequent issue in CKD, exacerbated by reduced renal function. Management includes dietary potassium restriction, use of kaliuretic diuretics, and avoiding potassium supplements or medications that retain potassium. Potassium-binding resins can also be used to promote gastrointestinal loss of potassium. In severe cases, dialysis may be necessary.
keyPoints: Hyperkalemia is common in CKD, worsened by reduced renal function.
Management includes dietary restriction, kaliuretic diuretics, and avoiding potassium supplements or retaining medications.
Potassium-binding resins can help reduce hyperkalemia through GI loss.
Intractable hyperkalemia may necessitate dialysis.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · FLUID, ELECTROLYTE, AND ACID-BASE DISORDERS (part 2) | Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Disorders

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Secondary Hyperparathyroidism in CKD
items.content: Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD is driven by declining GFR, leading to phosphate retention and increased PTH synthesis. This process involves elevated FGF-23 levels, reduced ionized calcium due to decreased calcitriol production, and attenuated Klotho-dependent inhibition of PTH.
keyPoints: Declining GFR below 60 mL/min initiates these changes.
Phosphate retention stimulates increased synthesis of FGF-23 and PTH.
High levels of FGF-23 are an independent risk factor for left ventricular hypertrophy and increased mortality in CKD patients.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Hyperparathyroidism
items.content: Severe hyperparathyroidism in CKD patients leads to osteitis fibrosa cystica, characterized by bone pain, fragility, and brown tumors. PTH is also a uremic toxin contributing to muscle weakness, cardiac fibrosis, and resistance to erythropoiesis-stimulating agents.
keyPoints: Bone histology shows abnormal osteoid, fibrosis, and cysts (brown tumors).
Clinical symptoms include bone pain, fragility, and compression syndromes.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: Association of Hyperphosphatemia with Cardiovascular Mortality in CKD
items.content: Hyperphosphatemia is strongly linked to increased cardiovascular mortality and vascular calcification in CKD patients. This association may be due to the inability of ingested calcium to incorporate into bones, leading to deposition at extraosseous sites.
keyPoints: CT scans show significant coronary artery and heart valve calcification in CKD patients.
Calcification magnitude correlates with age, hyperphosphatemia, low PTH levels, and low bone turnover.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 2) | Disorders of Calcium and Phosphate Metabolism
items.title: Understanding Calciphylaxis in CKD Patients
items.content: Calciphylaxis is a severe condition predominantly seen in advanced CKD patients, characterized by painful skin lesions progressing to ischemic necrosis. It involves vascular occlusion and extensive soft tissue calcification. While initially linked to hyperparathyroidism, it now occurs even without severe hyperparathyroidism. Warfarin use is a risk factor due to its impact on matrix GLA protein, which prevents vascular calcification.
keyPoints: Calciphylaxis is associated with advanced CKD and characterized by painful livedo reticularis and ischemic necrosis.
Pathology involves vascular occlusion and extensive soft tissue calcification.
Warfarin use decreases matrix GLA protein activation, increasing calciphylaxis risk; discontinue if syndrome develops.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 2) | Disorders of Calcium and Phosphate Metabolism

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 2) | Disorders of Calcium and Phosphate Metabolism
items.title: Management Strategies for Hyperphosphatemia in CKD
items.content: Managing hyperphosphatemia in CKD involves dietary phosphate restriction, use of phosphate binders, and careful monitoring. Calcium-based binders can lead to hypercalcemia, while non-calcium options like sevelamer and lanthanum avoid this risk. Calcimimetic agents such as cinacalcet reduce PTH levels by enhancing calcium sensitivity in parathyroid cells.
keyPoints: Phosphate binders include calcium acetate, calcium carbonate (risk of hypercalcemia), and non-calcium options like sevelamer and lanthanum.
Calcimimetics (e.g., cinacalcet) reduce PTH by enhancing parathyroid cell sensitivity to calcium.
NKF KDOQI guidelines recommend a target PTH level between 2-9 times the upper limit of normal.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DISORDERS OF CALCIUM AND PHOSPHATE METABOLISM (part 2) | Disorders of Calcium and Phosphate Metabolism

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.title: Elevated Cardiovascular Risk in CKD Patients
items.content: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality across all stages of chronic kidney disease (CKD). The risk of cardiovascular disease in CKD patients compared to age- and sex-matched individuals ranges from 10-fold to 200-fold, depending on the stage. Most CKD patients die from cardiovascular causes before reaching stage 5.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in CKD at all stages.
Incremental risk of cardiovascular disease in CKD ranges from 10- to 200-fold compared to matched general population.
Most patients with CKD die from cardiovascular causes before reaching stage 5.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.title: Diagnosing Acute Myocardial Infarction in CKD Patients
items.content: Cardiac troponin levels are often elevated in CKD patients without acute ischemia, complicating the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction. Serial measurements may be necessary to assess trends over time rather than relying on a single elevated level.
keyPoints: Elevated cardiac troponin levels can occur in CKD without acute ischemia.
Diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction is complicated by these elevations.
Serial measurements of troponin are more informative than a single measurement.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.title: Heart Failure and Pulmonary Edema in Advanced CKD
items.content: In advanced CKD, heart failure can occur with preserved or reduced ejection fraction. A unique form of 'low-pressure' pulmonary edema may manifest as shortness of breath and a 'bat wing' pattern on chest x-ray, often responding to dialysis.
keyPoints: Heart failure in CKD can present with either preserved or reduced ejection fraction.
'Low-pressure' pulmonary edema is characterized by a 'bat wing' distribution on chest x-ray.
This form of pulmonary edema responds to dialysis and may occur without ECFV overload.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Target BP Goals for CKD Patients with Diabetes or Proteinuria >1 g/24h
items.content: In CKD patients with diabetes or significant proteinuria, the recommended blood pressure target is <130/80 mmHg to prevent extrarenal complications like cardiovascular disease and stroke. This goal should be pursued if it can be achieved without causing prohibitive adverse effects.
keyPoints: Blood pressure target for CKD patients with diabetes or proteinuria >1 g/24h: <130/80 mmHg
Salt restriction is the first line of therapy
ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred due to their renal protective effects beyond blood pressure reduction
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGEMENT OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Monitoring and Adjusting RAS Inhibitors in CKD Patients with Low eGFR
items.content: In patients with progressive CKD, especially those with an eGFR <30 mL/min, ACE inhibitors and ARBs can be continued with careful monitoring. Discontinuation of these agents is associated with increased risk of death or cardiovascular events but a lower incidence of starting kidney replacement therapy.
keyPoints: RAS inhibitors (ACEi/ARBs) can continue in CKD patients even if eGFR <30 mL/min
Discontinuing RAS inhibitors increases risk of death/cardiovascular events
Hyperkalemia management: use kaliretic diuretics or potassium-lowering GI binders like patiromer
Avoid potassium-sparing diuretics and monitor serum potassium with mineralocorticoid receptor blockers
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGEMENT OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE | Pericardial Disease
items.title: Urgent Dialysis for Uremic Pericarditis in CKD Patients
items.content: Uremic pericarditis is a critical condition in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD) that necessitates immediate intervention. It serves as an absolute indication for the urgent initiation or intensification of dialysis, particularly because these patients are at risk of hemorrhage when treated with heparin during hemodialysis.
keyPoints: Uremic pericarditis is a critical condition requiring urgent dialysis in CKD patients
Hemorrhagic risk necessitates performing hemodialysis without heparin for these patients
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE | Pericardial Disease

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE | Pericardial Disease
items.title: Management of Recurrent Pericardial Effusion in CKD Patients
items.content: In CKD patients experiencing recurrent pericardial effusions, especially when echocardiographic signs suggest impending tamponade, a pericardial drainage procedure should be considered. This is crucial to prevent complications such as cardiac tamponade.
keyPoints: Consider pericardial drainage for recurrent effusion with signs of impending tamponade
Pericarditis in CKD can occur due to uremia, viral infections, malignancy, tuberculosis, autoimmune diseases, myocardial infarction, or minoxidil treatment
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE | Pericardial Disease

items.subtopic: HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia
items.title: Erythropoietin Deficiency and Its Consequences in CKD-Related Anemia
items.content: In chronic kidney disease (CKD), anemia typically presents as normocytic, normochromic due to insufficient erythropoietin production by damaged kidneys. This deficiency leads to decreased tissue oxygen delivery, increased cardiac output, ventricular dilation, and hypertrophy.
keyPoints: Anemia is observed from stage 3 CKD and becomes almost universal by stage 4.
Primary cause: Insufficient erythropoietin production.
Consequences include fatigue, diminished exercise tolerance, angina, heart failure, decreased cognition, impaired host defense against infection, and growth restriction in children.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia

items.subtopic: HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia
items.title: Role of Recombinant Human Erythropoiesis-Stimulating Agents (ESAs) in CKD
items.content: Recombinant human ESAs have significantly improved the management of anemia in renal patients by reducing reliance on blood transfusions, thereby decreasing associated risks like infections and iron overload. Before ESA treatment, adequate bone marrow iron stores are essential.
keyPoints: ESAs reduce need for regular blood transfusions.
Iron supplementation is crucial before ESA initiation to ensure optimal response.
Target hemoglobin concentration: 100–115 g/L.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia

items.subtopic: HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia
items.title: Potential Risks and Considerations with ESA Therapy in CKD Patients
items.content: While ESAs are effective in managing anemia, randomized trials have not shown cardiovascular benefits. There is a potential increased risk of stroke in type 2 diabetics and thromboembolic events, necessitating careful balancing of symptom improvement against these risks.
keyPoints: No demonstrated improvement in cardiovascular outcomes with ESA therapy.
Potential increased risk of stroke in CKD patients with type 2 diabetes.
Possible increase in thromboembolic events and faster renal decline progression.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia

items.subtopic: Abnormal Hemostasis | NEUROMUSCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.title: Management of Coagulopathy in CKD Patients
items.content: In patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), managing coagulopathy involves balancing the risk of bleeding and thrombosis. Desmopressin, cryoprecipitate, IV conjugated estrogens, blood transfusions, and ESA therapy can temporarily reverse abnormal bleeding time and coagulopathy. Optimal dialysis often corrects prolonged bleeding time. However, anticoagulation decisions must be individualized due to the unique risk profile in CKD patients, particularly with warfarin for atrial fibrillation, as there is a higher risk of bleeding complications and potential precipitating calciphylaxis.
keyPoints: Desmopressin (DDAVP), cryoprecipitate, IV conjugated estrogens, blood transfusions, and ESA therapy can temporarily reverse coagulopathy in CKD patients.
Optimal dialysis usually corrects prolonged bleeding time.
Warfarin anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation requires individualized decision-making due to increased risk of bleeding complications and calciphylaxis in advanced CKD.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Abnormal Hemostasis | NEUROMUSCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: Abnormal Hemostasis | NEUROMUSCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.title: Neuromuscular Complications in CKD
items.content: CKD patients often experience neuromuscular abnormalities, including CNS, peripheral, and autonomic neuropathy. Subtle manifestations of uremic neuromuscular disease appear at stage 3 CKD, with early CNS symptoms like memory disturbances, concentration issues, and sleep disturbances. Peripheral neuropathy becomes clinically evident in stage 4 CKD, initially affecting sensory nerves more than motor ones, and lower extremities more than upper. The presence of peripheral neuropathy without other causes (e.g., diabetes mellitus or iron deficiency) indicates the need for renal replacement therapy.
keyPoints: Neuromuscular abnormalities in CKD include CNS, peripheral, and autonomic neuropathy.
Subtle manifestations appear at stage 3 CKD with early CNS symptoms like memory disturbances and sleep issues.
Peripheral neuropathy becomes clinically evident in stage 4 CKD, affecting sensory nerves more than motor ones initially.
Evidence of peripheral neuropathy without other causes indicates the need for renal replacement therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Abnormal Hemostasis | NEUROMUSCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES
items.title: Management of Nutritional Issues in CKD Patients
items.content: CKD patients often experience gastrointestinal and nutritional abnormalities such as uremic fetor, gastritis, peptic disease, and constipation. Protein restriction can help manage nausea but risks malnutrition. Weight loss and protein-energy malnutrition are indicators for renal replacement therapy initiation.
keyPoints: Uremic fetor results from urea breakdown to ammonia in saliva.
Protein restriction may reduce nausea but increases malnutrition risk.
Weight loss and protein-energy malnutrition indicate potential need for renal replacement therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES
items.title: Endocrine-Metabolic Considerations in CKD: Glucose Metabolism and Insulin Management
items.content: CKD impairs glucose metabolism, with fasting blood glucose usually normal or slightly elevated. Plasma insulin levels are higher due to reduced renal degradation, necessitating dose adjustments for patients on insulin therapy as renal function declines.
keyPoints: Fasting blood glucose is typically normal or only slightly elevated in CKD.
Plasma insulin levels are elevated due to diminished renal degradation.
Insulin doses may need reduction as renal function worsens.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES
items.title: Reproductive and Hormonal Implications in CKD Patients
items.content: CKD affects reproductive health, with low estrogen levels leading to menstrual abnormalities and infertility in women. Men experience reduced testosterone and sexual dysfunction. Pregnancy is risky when GFR declines to ~40 mL/min, with only ~20% resulting in live births.
keyPoints: Women with CKD have low estrogen levels and high spontaneous abortion rates at GFR ~40 mL/min.
Only ~20% of pregnancies result in live births at this GFR level.
Men with CKD experience reduced testosterone and sexual dysfunction.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · GASTROINTESTINAL AND NUTRITIONAL ABNORMALITIES | ENDOCRINE-METABOLIC DISTURBANCES

items.subtopic: DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)
items.title: Management of Pruritus in CKD
items.content: Pruritus is a common and challenging symptom in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), often persisting even on dialysis. Initial management should focus on ruling out unrelated skin disorders like scabies and addressing hyperphosphatemia, which can contribute to itching.
keyPoints: Pruritus is prevalent in CKD and may continue despite dialysis.
Rule out unrelated skin conditions such as scabies.
Treat hyperphosphatemia to alleviate pruritus.
Use local moisturizers, mild topical glucocorticoids, oral antihistamines, and ultraviolet radiation for relief.
Kappa opioid receptor agonists can reduce pruritus intensity.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)

items.subtopic: DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)
items.title: Nephrogenic Fibrosing Dermopathy and Gadolinium Exposure
items.content: Nephrogenic fibrosing dermopathy is a rare skin condition in CKD patients, often linked to exposure to gadolinium-based contrast agents. Patients with advanced CKD should minimize or avoid gadolinium unless critical for imaging.
keyPoints: Nephrogenic fibrosing dermopathy occurs rarely and involves subcutaneous induration.
Associated with gadolinium exposure in patients with CKD stages 3-5.
CKD stage 3 (GFR 30–59 mL/min): minimize gadolinium use.
CKD stages 4-5 (GFR <30 mL/min): avoid gadolinium unless medically necessary.
Rapid hemodialysis post-gadolinium exposure may mitigate complications.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)

items.subtopic: DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)
items.title: Initial Approach to Diagnosing CKD
items.content: Diagnosis of CKD often occurs unexpectedly due to subtle symptoms. A thorough history and physical examination are crucial, focusing on risk factors like hypertension, diabetes, and drug exposure.
keyPoints: CKD diagnosis may be surprising due to subtle initial symptoms.
History should include hypertension, diabetes, abnormal urinalyses, and relevant drug use.
Evaluate for uremic syndrome through questions about appetite, weight loss, nausea, edema, muscle cramps, pruritus, and restless legs.
Family history and organ system manifestations can indicate heritable CKD forms.
Physical exam should assess blood pressure, target organ damage, and signs like asterixis or pericardial friction rub.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · DERMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | INITIAL APPROACH (part 1)

items.subtopic: INITIAL APPROACH (part 2)
items.title: When and How to Use Renal Ultrasound in CKD
items.content: Renal ultrasound is pivotal for assessing kidney size, symmetry, and ruling out masses or obstruction. Bilaterally small kidneys suggest chronic disease, while normal size may indicate acute/subacute conditions. Exceptions include diabetic nephropathy (initially enlarged kidneys), amyloidosis, and HIV nephropathy with normal-sized kidneys despite CKD.
keyPoints: Renal ultrasound verifies two kidneys, symmetry, estimates kidney size, rules out masses/obstruction.
Bilaterally small kidneys indicate long-standing CKD; normal size suggests acute/subacute disease.
Exceptions: Diabetic nephropathy (initially enlarged), amyloidosis, HIV nephropathy with normal-sized kidneys.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · INITIAL APPROACH (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL APPROACH (part 2)
items.title: Guidelines for Kidney Biopsy in CKD Patients
items.content: Renal biopsy is contraindicated in patients with bilaterally small kidneys due to technical difficulty, high scarring obscuring diagnosis, and missed therapeutic window. Contraindications include uncontrolled hypertension, active UTI, bleeding diathesis, severe obesity. Ultrasound-guided percutaneous biopsy is preferred; hemodialysis without heparin may normalize bleeding time pre-biopsy.
keyPoints: Renal biopsy not advised for bilaterally small kidneys due to technical difficulty and high scarring.
Contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension, active UTI, bleeding diathesis, severe obesity.
Ultrasound-guided percutaneous biopsy preferred; hemodialysis without heparin may normalize bleeding time pre-biopsy.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · INITIAL APPROACH (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ ESTABLISHING THE DIAGNOSIS AND ETIOLOGY OF CKD
items.title: Distinguishing CKD from Acute Renal Failure
items.content: The initial diagnostic step in evaluating renal dysfunction is to differentiate between chronic kidney disease (CKD) and acute or subacute renal failure. This distinction is crucial as the latter conditions may respond to targeted therapy, whereas CKD typically does not. Historical serum creatinine levels are instrumental; normal values from recent months suggest a more acute and potentially reversible condition.
keyPoints: Previous serum creatinine measurements help distinguish between chronic and acute renal failure.
Normal past serum creatinine suggests potential reversibility of current renal dysfunction.
Elevated past serum creatinine indicates chronicity, but acute processes may be superimposed.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · ☑ ESTABLISHING THE DIAGNOSIS AND ETIOLOGY OF CKD

items.subtopic: ☑ ESTABLISHING THE DIAGNOSIS AND ETIOLOGY OF CKD
items.title: Indications for Kidney Biopsy in Early-Stage CKD
items.content: While kidney biopsy can be performed in early-stage CKD (stages 1–3), it is not always necessary. For instance, in patients with long-standing type 1 diabetes mellitus and nephrotic-range proteinuria without hematuria or retinopathy, diabetic nephropathy is likely, negating the need for biopsy. However, atypical findings such as hematuria or white blood cell casts may indicate another disease, warranting a biopsy.
keyPoints: Biopsy in early CKD (stages 1–3) is not always indicated.
Diabetic nephropathy diagnosis likely with long-standing type 1 diabetes and nephrotic-range proteinuria without hematuria or retinopathy.
Atypical findings like hematuria or white blood cell casts may necessitate a biopsy.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · ☑ ESTABLISHING THE DIAGNOSIS AND ETIOLOGY OF CKD

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD
items.title: Importance of Early Intervention in CKD to Prevent GFR Decline
items.content: Early intervention is crucial for patients with CKD, particularly before a measurable decline in Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) occurs. This approach involves identifying and treating reversible factors that may accelerate kidney injury.
keyPoints: Optimal timing for specific therapies is usually well before a measurable decline in GFR.
Sequential measurement and plotting of GFR rate of decline are recommended to detect any acceleration.

Potential reversible causes include ECFV depletion, uncontrolled hypertension, urinary tract infection, new obstructive uropathy, exposure to nephrotoxic agents (e.g., NSAIDs), and reactivation or flare of diseases like lupus or vasculitis.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD
items.title: Targeting Intraglomerular Hypertension and Proteinuria in CKD Management
items.content: Reducing intraglomerular hypertension and proteinuria is vital for slowing the progression of CKD. Effective management includes controlling blood pressure to reduce protein excretion, which correlates with protection from GFR decline.
keyPoints: Target blood pressure for proteinuric CKD patients is established at 130/80 mmHg.
ACE inhibitors and ARBs are effective in reducing glomerular hypertension and slowing renal failure progression.
Avoid combining ACE inhibitors and ARBs due to increased risk of AKI and adverse cardiac events.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD
items.title: Role of Gliflozins in Managing CKD Progression
items.content: Gliflozins, by inhibiting SGLT2 transporters, activate tubuloglomerular feedback to reduce intraglomerular pressure and slow GFR decline. They also offer cardiovascular benefits.
keyPoints: Gliflozins result in afferent vasoconstriction and efferent vasodilation, reducing intraglomerular pressure.
Indicated for slowing GFR decline in both diabetic and nondiabetic kidney disease.
Demonstrated to reduce major cardiovascular events in CKD patients.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · Chronic Kidney Disease | SLOWING THE PROGRESSION OF CKD

items.subtopic: MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY
items.title: Medication Dose Adjustment in CKD
items.content: In chronic kidney disease (CKD), dose adjustment of medications is crucial due to altered drug clearance. Agents primarily eliminated by non-renal routes (>70%) may not require dosage changes, while those cleared renally need careful management.
keyPoints: Avoid metformin and meperidine in CKD due to renal excretion risks.
NSAIDs should be avoided as they can worsen kidney function.
Use nephrotoxic agents like radiocontrast and gadolinium according to strict guidelines.
Online databases are available for dose adjustment based on CKD stage or estimated GFR.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY

items.subtopic: MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY
items.title: Indications for Renal Replacement Therapy in CKD
items.content: Renal replacement therapy is indicated when patients with CKD exhibit symptoms like anorexia, nausea, malnutrition, and refractory fluid/electrolyte abnormalities. Encephalopathy and pericarditis are late complications rarely used as initiation triggers.
keyPoints: Indications include anorexia, nausea not from reversible causes, malnutrition, hyperkalemia, or ECFV overload.
Encephalopathy and pericarditis are rare indications for starting therapy.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY

items.subtopic: MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY
items.title: Optimal Timing and Education for Renal Replacement Therapy Initiation
items.content: Initiating renal replacement therapy should consider individual variability in uremic symptoms. Early preparation can prevent complications related to dialysis access. Patient education, starting at stage 4 CKD, enhances decision-making and quality of life.
keyPoints: Avoid arbitrary creatinine or urea nitrogen levels for dialysis initiation.
Early patient education improves outcomes and supports home-based therapy choices.
Education should begin no later than stage 4 CKD.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY

items.subtopic: IMPLICATIONS FOR GLOBAL HEALTH
items.title: Rising CKD Burden Due to Metabolic Disease Growth
items.content: The prevalence of metabolic and vascular diseases, including diabetes mellitus, is increasing in developing countries due to lifestyle changes. This trend predicts a significant rise in chronic kidney disease (CKD) burden globally.
keyPoints: Forecasted increase in global years of life lost (YLLs) due to CKD from ~26 million in 2016 to 52.5 million by 2040.
Mortality from CKD expected to rise from 1.2 million in 2016 to 3.1 million in 2040.
CKD projected to move from 16th to 5th place in YLL rankings by 2040.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IMPLICATIONS FOR GLOBAL HEALTH

items.subtopic: IMPLICATIONS FOR GLOBAL HEALTH
items.title: Endemic Nephropathies and CKD in Developing Countries
items.content: Developing countries are witnessing a rise in endemic nephropathies, particularly affecting young males in agriculture. These conditions contribute to the increasing CKD burden.
keyPoints: Endemic nephropathies disproportionately affect young males working in agriculture.
Potential contributing factors include genetic risk, exposure to nephrotoxins and pesticides, NSAID use, chronic volume depletion, global warming, and air pollution.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · IMPLICATIONS FOR GLOBAL HEALTH

items.assertion: CKD patients with ECFV expansion should be counseled regarding salt restriction.
items.reason: Salt restriction helps manage sodium retention and prevent hypertension.

items.assertion: Hyperkalemia in CKD is primarily due to decreased urinary potassium excretion.
items.reason: The decline in GFR does not necessarily lead to a parallel decline in urinary potassium excretion.

items.assertion: Hyperparathyroidism in CKD leads to osteitis fibrosa cystica.
items.reason: Bone histology shows abnormal osteoid and bone marrow fibrosis, leading to brown tumors.

items.assertion: Adynamic bone disease is less prevalent among diabetics.
items.reason: It results from excessive suppression of PTH production and chronic inflammation.

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.assertion: Anemia is a risk factor for heart failure in CKD patients.
items.reason: Anemia contributes to the high cardiac output state, increasing the risk of heart failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with anemia contributing to heart failure through increased cardiac output.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR ABNORMALITIES
items.assertion: Left ventricular hypertrophy is primarily due to hyperlipidemia in CKD patients.
items.reason: Hypertension and ECFV overload are the primary causes of left ventricular hypertrophy in CKD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hypertension and ECFV overload, not hyperlipidemia, primarily cause left ventricular hypertrophy.

items.subtopic: HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia
items.assertion: Iron supplementation is essential before initiating ESA treatment in CKD patients.
items.reason: The demand for iron by the marrow frequently exceeds the amount of iron that is immediately available for erythropoiesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why iron supplementation is essential.

items.assertion: Prolyl-hydroxylase inhibitors increase endogenous EPO production.
items.reason: They lead to an increase in GI absorption of iron and EPO production.

items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease · HEMATOLOGIC ABNORMALITIES | Anemia

items.assertion: The restless leg syndrome is characterized by discomfort in the legs and feet relieved by movement.

items.assertion: Glifozins reduce kidney function decline in CKD patients.

items.assertion: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are effective in slowing the progression of renal failure.
items.reason: They achieve this through efferent vasodilatation, which reduces glomerular hypertension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ACE inhibitors and ARBs slow renal failure progression.

items.assertion: Calcium channel blockers like diltiazem and verapamil are less effective than dihydropyridines in reducing proteinuria.
items.reason: They exhibit superior antiproteinuric and renoprotective effects compared to the dihydropyridines.

items.assertion: Patient education should be initiated no later than stage 4 CKD.
items.reason: This timing allows patients sufficient time and cognitive function to learn important concepts, make informed choices, and implement preparatory measures for renal replacement therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: MANAGING OTHER COMPLICATIONS OF CKD | PREPARATION FOR RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY
items.assertion: Preemptive kidney transplantation is preferred over transplanting after a period of dialysis.
items.reason: Preemptive transplantation avoids the complications associated with temporary hemodialysis access, such as sepsis and accelerated mortality.
